title: Walking in Love, Part 2
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Walking in Love, Part 2 - Pastor Tito Lyro - Ephesians 5_1-8a.txt
outline: Be imitators of God as beloved children
Walking in love as Christ loved us and gave Himself up
Sexual immorality and impurity must not even be named among you
Covetousness as a form of idolatry
Walking as children of light, not of darkness
Conclusion: The high calling to walk in love and light
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro continues the Ephesians series with the second part of a sermon on Ephesians 5:1-8a, where Paul calls believers to be imitators of God and walk in love as Christ loved us. He addresses the incompatibility of sexual immorality, impurity, and covetousness with the calling to walk as children of light.
transcript:
## Introduction: Walk in Love

I titled this sermon "Walking in Love, Part 2," and you might wonder where part 1 is. Part 1 was preached on February 16th of this year. I trust you all remember it, so we can pick up from here.

As we come to chapter 5 verse 1, Paul picks up what he began in chapter 4 verse 1. He ties the language of "walking" throughout: in chapter 4 verse 1 Paul urges us to "walk worthy of the calling with which you were called"; in chapter 5 he develops that idea and tells us in verse 1 to **walk in love**, in verse 8 to **walk as light**, and in verse 15 to **walk carefully**. Having laid the foundation of the Christian faith in chapters 1 through 3 — God prepared a plan before the world began (ch. 1), executed it through Jesus Christ (ch. 2), and applied it by the Spirit bringing Jew and Gentile into one body (ch. 3) — Paul says in chapter 4: because of all those truths, **walk** in a certain way.

Notice that Paul puts the Christian faith in terms of walking. He does not say "sit in love" or "rest in love." He says **walk** in love — which tells us the Christian faith is an active faith, progressive and perhaps sometimes slow. He does not say "run in love" or "sprint in love" either. He says walk — the constant, everyday living in light of the love of Christ for us. Our sanctification is a work in which God gives us grace and we cooperate with that grace. As Paul writes in Philippians 2:12–13:

> *"Work out your own salvation with fear and trembling; for it is God who works in you both to will and to do for his good pleasure."* (Philippians 2:12–13)

You may have heard the expression "let go and let God." It sounds pious, but it is not biblical. We are not supposed to let go and let God. We are supposed to let God — and then work really hard. Both are true in our sanctification.

## The Pattern for Walking in Love: Christ's Sacrifice

Notice also that all this walking in chapter 5 is the description of how we **imitate God**:

> *"Therefore be imitators of God as dear children, and walk in love, as Christ also has loved us and given himself for us, an offering and a sacrifice to God for a sweet-smelling aroma."* (Ephesians 5:1–2)

The word "imitator" is from the Greek *mimetes* — where we get the word *mimic*. And in telling us how to imitate God, Paul describes that imitation concretely. Walk in love. Walk as light. Walk carefully. Each of those comes with specific, practical direction on how to imitate God — in the way we believe, the way we think, the way we act, and the way we feel.

The pattern of the love we are to walk in is **Christ's love for us**. Paul describes it here as Christ's giving himself for us as an offering and a sacrifice. This sacrifice has two aspects.

**First, it is a substitutionary sacrifice.** When Christ died, he died the death we as sinners deserved. He died in our place. If you believe he did that for you, that is what it means to trust in the substitutionary atonement. And because of his offering, the Father looks at those who believe and says: you are forgiven. As Paul writes in 2 Corinthians 5:21:

> *"For he made him who knew no sin to be sin for us, that we might become the righteousness of God in him."* (2 Corinthians 5:21)

Notice the phrase "sweet-smelling aroma" at the end of verse 2. The Father was pleased with Christ's substitutionary sacrifice on our behalf. When Christ offered himself, it was a sweet-smelling aroma in the Father's sight.

**Second, it is an example for us.** Some liberal theologians in the early twentieth century denied that Christ died for sinners and reduced the cross to only an example to follow. Conservatives, in reacting against that error, have sometimes forgotten that Christ is also our example. He is both. We are to look at his self-giving love and follow it.

Paul uses Christ's giving of himself as a pattern for us in two ways. First, in general terms, in the great Christ-hymn of Philippians 2:5–11: "Let this mind be in you, which was also in Christ Jesus, who, being in the form of God, did not consider equality with God something to be grasped, but humbled himself and became obedient to death, even death on the cross." The point of that passage is in the verses leading up to it — Philippians 2:2–4 — where Paul says: consider others; let not only your own interests drive you, but the interests of others also; be humble before God and serve one another. Christ was God himself. He did not think it was beneath him to serve you. Why should we ever think God is asking too much when he calls us to walk in love by giving ourselves to other sinners just like us?

Second, Paul uses this example specifically for husbands:

> *"Husbands, love your wives, just as Christ also loved the church and gave himself for her."* (Ephesians 5:25)

A husband might say: but you do not know my wife. Well — do you think God knew you, husband? Did he know the darkness and sinfulness of your heart, and still give his Son for you? That is the pattern you are to follow. A husband cannot walk in love and fail to love his wife selflessly. Those two things cannot coexist.

> *"This is my commandment, that you love one another as I have loved you. Greater love has no one than this, than to lay down one's life for his friends."* (John 15:12–13)

That is the way Christ sets for us — exemplary selflessness by giving ourselves to others.

And to walk in love in this way, to offer ourselves as a living sacrifice to others, is — as Paul says in Romans 12:1 — our "reasonable service." The word *reasonable* there is actually the word *logical*. It is your logical service. If Christ is your Savior, you cannot escape the logic that you are going to live a life dedicated to him. And all your life will be a service of worship — walking in love as a logical conclusion of following Christ.

## What Walking in Love Does Not Include

Paul now states negatively what is incompatible with walking in love. He gives two lists of three in verses 3 and 4.

**List 1 (verse 3): Fornication, uncleanness, covetousness**

> *"But fornication and all uncleanness or covetousness, let it not even be named among you, as is fitting for saints."* (Ephesians 5:3)

The "but" is a contrast. These are the anti-love actions — things that are contrary to walking in love and therefore not to be found among those who claim to follow Christ.

*Fornication* — sexual activity by unmarried people. The word covers all sexual immorality. Some younger people in romantic relationships think the ultimate demonstration of their love for one another is to have sex. But Paul says premarital sexual relations are the very opposite of love according to Scripture.

> *"For this is the will of God, your sanctification: that each of you should abstain from sexual immorality."* (1 Thessalonians 4:3)

*Uncleanness* (impurity) — Paul broadens the category beyond the physical act to include all sexual sins. Some Christians think they can walk right up to the line without crossing it. Paul says: nobody parks on the on-ramp of the freeway and stays there. This term includes the use of pornography. Walking in love and using pornography cannot coexist. I am not saying you must be morally perfect. But the thought that "I can be both a pornography user and a Christian at the same time" must not be entertained.

*Covetousness* — wanting what others have to the point of being willing to sin in order to get it. This is the ultimate anti-love in the list, and the broadest — it reaches into the realm of thoughts. Covetousness is the displacement of Jesus from the center of the heart and the substitution of an idol in his place. That is why Paul elsewhere equates covetousness with idolatry: "covetousness, which is idolatry" (Colossians 3:5). The first and last commandments are united together in this.

**List 2 (verse 4): Filthiness, foolish talk, coarse jesting**

> *"Neither filthiness, nor foolish talking, nor coarse jesting, which are not fitting; but rather giving of thanks."* (Ephesians 5:4)

*Filthiness* — obscenity. There is a movement in some Christian circles to eliminate shame entirely. But the Bible says we should be ashamed of sinning. Shame is part of repentance. Paul says the saint's life will not be characterized by obscenity.

*Foolish talking* — literally, moronic words. The fool of Proverbs is the man who opens his mouth before thinking, who treats serious things carelessly. We are the wise of Proverbs, not the fools. Our speech should reflect that.

*Coarse jesting* — crude jokes, laughing at what God calls sin. The Christian does not find entertainment in what God calls wicked.

Then Paul offers the positive alternative: **giving of thanks**. It seems simple, but it is real. You fight covetousness by giving thanks — by focusing on the blessings God has given you rather than on what you think you lack. You fight sexual sin by giving thanks — by focusing on God's perfect design for sexuality and being grateful for it. Above all, you have Christ. You have eternal life. No matter how difficult your circumstances — a troubled marriage, rebellious children, the daily struggle with sin — you have Christ, and that is reason for infinite gratitude.

> *"For our light and momentary troubles are achieving for us an eternal glory that far outweighs them all."* (2 Corinthians 4:17)

Instead of wanting what you do not have and cannot have, walk in love by being thankful for what Christ has done.

## Identity: Children of Light, Not Sons of Disobedience

Why does Paul make such a big deal about all this? Because it is a matter of identity.

> *"For this you know, that no fornicator, unclean person, nor covetous man, who is an idolater, has any inheritance in the kingdom of Christ and God."* (Ephesians 5:5)

Paul is not telling them anything new. *You know this.* A citizen of heaven, one who has inherited the kingdom of Christ and God through adoption as a son of God, is not identified by these sins. You have an identity — child of God, redeemed by Jesus Christ. And if that is your identity, then these other things are not your identity. You are either a fornicator, a covetous person, a foolish talker — or you are a child of God. You cannot be identified as both.

This does not mean you must be morally perfect. But it does mean that if you are a believer, you will not live as a fornicator or think of yourself as one. You will see yourself as a child of God. And whatever of these things may be present in your life, you will be ashamed of them, repent of them, and turn away from them.

> *"Let no one deceive you with empty words, for because of these things the wrath of God comes upon the sons of disobedience."* (Ephesians 5:6)

There is such a thing as being so open-minded that your brain falls out. There are things we are not supposed to be open-minded about. Sexual sin is not a matter of indifference. The wrath of God comes upon those who practice these things — and upon those who applaud them. That is how serious this is.

## Conclusion: Walk as Children of Light

> *"Therefore do not be partakers with them; for you were once darkness, but now you are light in the Lord."* (Ephesians 5:7–8a)

Notice that Paul does not say we are *in* the light. He says we *are* light — as he writes in Philippians 2, we are like stars shining in the darkness. We follow Jesus in that light.

So instead of walking in the darkness of these sinful behaviors Paul has described, we who are redeemed by Christ walk in love. We walk in humble selflessness, giving of ourselves to those around us. We walk in Christ alone, by the power of the cross, seeking to imitate him in our lives.
